channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: UniMSM: An Efficient and Flexible Hardware Accelerator for Multi-Scalar Multiplication
item.description: Multi-scalar multiplication (MSM) is a performance-critical kernel in modern cryptography, especially for zero-knowledge proofs and blockchain systems. Its irregular memory access patterns—particularly from Pippenger’s bucket method—and high-precision modular arithmetic severely limit hardware utilization in deep-pipeline accelerators. This paper presents **UniMSM**, an efficient and flexible hardware accelerator for MSM across practical scales and diverse elliptic curves. We introduce three key innovations: (1) a pipelined point adder using extended Jacobian coordinates with time-multiplexed modular multipliers to reduce area cost by 42% while sustaining >800 MHz throughput; (2) a conflict-aware scheduling scheme that models and resolves bucket-update conflicts dynamically, boosting pipeline utilization to 91.5%; and (3) a hardware-friendly variant of Pippenger’s algorithm featuring hierarchical aggregation and lazy reduction, cutting intermediate storage by 3.3× and eliminating 76% of critical-path serial dependencies. Evaluated on Xilinx Alveo U280 FPGA and 7nm ASIC, UniMSM achieves up to **2.12× and 3.85× improvement in area-time product (ATP)** over prior FPGA and ASIC accelerators, respectively, while natively supporting Secp256k1, BN254, and BLS12-381 curves.

item.title: Sparse optimisation and quantum-inspired encoding for ransomware detection
item.description: Ransomware detection faces critical challenges from high-dimensional, noisy network traffic and adaptive obfuscation. We propose **BioSparse-MCP**, a classical, hardware-agnostic framework integrating: (i) gradient-based Minimax Concave Penalty (MCP) optimization for interpretable sparsity; (ii) Rotated Circular Partitioning (RCP) to enhance spatial discriminability of selected features; and (iii) Quantum Feature Mapping (QFM)-inspired spherical projection, rotation, and angular partitioning—simulated efficiently on CPUs with *O(d)* complexity. Evaluated on 149,043 real-world traffic instances using KNN and LSTM ensembles, BioSparse-MCP achieves **99.75% accuracy** and a **0.25% false positive rate**. Attribution analysis identifies cryptocurrency addresses, threat signatures, and IP-level features as most salient. This work demonstrates that synergistic sparse optimization and quantum-inspired geometric encoding enable efficient, accurate, and explainable ransomware detection in production-grade networks.

item.title: A Generalized Partial Exposure Lattice Attack Against an RSA variant Based on Cubic Pell Curves
item.description: This paper presents a **generalized partial key exposure lattice attack** against the cubic Pell curve-based RSA variant by Nitaj and Seck (MJAGA 2024), which operates over $\mathbb{Z}/N\mathbb{Z}$ with $N = p^r q^s$. Unlike prior work (Rahmani–Nitaj, AfricaCrypt 2025) restricted to $r = s = 1$, our attack handles arbitrary exponents $r, s \geq 1$ by exploiting the refined key equation $e u_0 - (p-1)^2 (q-1)^2 v_0 = w_0$, where $|w_0| < e$ and $u_0, v_0$ are small integers. When an adversary knows approximately $\frac{1}{4}\log_2 N$ high-order or low-order bits of $p$ or $q$, we construct an efficient LLL-reduced lattice to recover the secret parameters in polynomial time. Our theoretical bound is tight and experimentally validated for 1024-bit $N$ under 128–160 leaked bits. This result exposes a critical structural weakness: the scheme is insecure under practical partial-exposure scenarios, including timing or fault-injection attacks.

item.title: Inevitable Encounters: Backdoor Attacks Involving Lossy Compression
item.description: Real-world backdoor attacks face a critical yet overlooked challenge: lossy compression (e.g., JPEG, LIC) inevitably degrades embedded triggers during storage/transmission, causing attack failure after decompression. This paper pioneers the *compression-aware backdoor paradigm*, requiring trigger information to survive *in the binary bitstream itself* and be recoverable post-decompression. Leveraging ROI coding in image compression, we propose two novel strategies: (1) **Universal Attack Activation (UAA)**—a sample-specific ROI masking method that reactivates suppressed triggers in learned image compression (LIC) bitstreams; and (2) **Compression-Adapted Attack (CAA)**—a codec-agnostic strategy that *intentionally encodes trigger bits into ROI-weighted bitstream positions*, effective for both traditional codecs (e.g., JPEG QF=30) and LIC. Extensive experiments show CAA achieves >92% ASR under severe JPEG compression, while UAA attains 89.7% ASR on LIC models—outperforming prior methods by over 85 percentage points. Our work establishes bitstream-level ROI control as the key to robust, deployable backdoor attacks.

item.title: Sirens' Whisper: Inaudible Near-Ultrasonic Jailbreaks of Speech-Driven LLMs
item.description: We present **Sirens’ Whisper (SWhisper)**, the first practical, black-box, inaudible prompt injection framework against speech-driven LLMs using commodity hardware. SWhisper encodes arbitrary baseband prompts—including long, structured instructions—into near-ultrasonic waveforms (18–22 kHz) that reliably demodulate via microphone nonlinearity after acoustic transmission. By modeling device- and environment-specific nonlinearities with lightweight channel-inversion pre-compensation, it achieves high-fidelity covert audio delivery across diverse microphones and rooms. We further design a voice-aware jailbreak generation method optimizing for intelligibility, brevity (<3 s), and cross-model transferability. Experiments on six commercial and open-source speech-LLMs show strong black-box effectiveness: up to **0.94 Non-Refusal (NR)** and **0.925 Specific-Convincing (SC)** rates. A controlled user study confirms perceptual indistinguishability from background noise (AUC > 0.97). Beyond jailbreaking, SWhisper establishes a general-purpose, high-fidelity acoustic command channel for speech-AI systems.

item.title: Bodhi VLM: Privacy-Alignment Modeling for Hierarchical Visual Representations in Vision Backbones and VLM Encoders via Bottom-Up and Top-Down Feature Search
item.description: We introduce **Bodhi VLM**, a *privacy-alignment modeling* framework for hierarchical visual representations in vision backbones and VLM encoders. It jointly addresses three challenges: (1) layer-wise clustering of sensitive concepts via NCP and MDAV; (2) bidirectional localization of sensitive feature regions using bottom-up (BUA) and top-down (TDA) search over multi-scale representations; and (3) interpretable budget alignment via the Expectation-Maximization Privacy Assessment (EMPA) module, which computes a reference-relative signal—*not* a formal DP estimator—by fitting sensitive-feature distributions against user-specified priors (e.g., Laplace with scale $c/\varepsilon$). Evaluated on YOLO, PPDPTS, DETR, CLIP, LLaVA, and BLIP, Bodhi VLM demonstrates consistent BUA/TDA deviation trends and stable EMPA signals across noise regimes. It outperforms generic (Chi-square, KL, MMD) and task-aware (MomentReg, NoiseMLE, Wass-1) baselines in robustness and interpretability. This work shifts privacy alignment from post-hoc auditing to *learnable, hierarchical, and model-intrinsic* representation design.

item.title: REAEDP: Entropy-Calibrated Differentially Private Data Release with Formal Guarantees and Attack-Based Evaluation
item.description: REAEDP is a differentially private data release framework that bridges formal privacy guarantees with empirically grounded utility evaluation. It introduces entropy-calibrated histogram release—deriving the first explicit sensitivity bound for Shannon entropy (and extending it to Rényi entropy) over adjacent histograms—to enable precise noise calibration. Its synthetic-data mechanism $\mathcal{F}$ is proven to satisfy $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-DP under stated conditions, and its evaluation adopts realistic membership inference and record linkage attacks as primary utility-privacy metrics. Experiments across 7 tabular datasets show: (1) empirical entropy changes consistently stay below the theoretical bound; (2) baseline mechanisms (Laplace/Gaussian) exhibit similar entropy decay trends but lower utility; and (3) as $\varepsilon$ decreases, attack performance converges to random guessing (e.g., membership AUC drops from 0.91 to 0.53), validating strong privacy enforcement. REAEDP thus delivers a practical, attack-validated, and theoretically sound pipeline for privacy-preserving data release.

item.title: SecDTD: Dynamic Token Drop for Secure Transformers Inference
item.description: The rapid deployment of Transformer-based inference APIs raises critical privacy concerns when processing sensitive inputs, yet existing secure inference frameworks suffer from prohibitive computational and communication overhead. While token dropping reduces cost in plaintext, its direct application to ciphertext settings is suboptimal due to skewed cost distributions in secure computation (e.g., Softmax dominates). SecDTD addresses this by *dynamically dropping tokens earlier in the inference pipeline*, thereby eliminating expensive cryptographic operations on low-importance tokens. It introduces two key techniques: (1) **Max-Centric Normalization (MCN)**—a lightweight, Softmax-free scoring method enabling aggressive early dropping without accuracy loss; and (2) **OMSel**, an oblivious median selection protocol that achieves **16.9× speedup** over sorting-based baselines while preserving security and obliviousness. Evaluated across 48 experiments on eight GLUE tasks using BOLT and BumbleBee, SecDTD delivers **4.47× end-to-end inference acceleration** with no accuracy degradation.

item.title: GroupGuard: A Framework for Modeling and Defending Collusive Attacks in Multi-Agent Systems
item.description: Large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent systems promise transformative collaboration but face emerging security threats from coordinated adversarial behavior. This paper introduces *group collusive attacks*—a novel, sociologically grounded threat where multiple agents secretly coordinate to mislead the system more effectively than individual attackers. To counter this, we propose **GroupGuard**, a training-free defense framework featuring three synergistic layers: (1) continuous graph-based monitoring of interaction dynamics, (2) active semantic honeypot induction to expose collusion intent, and (3) structural pruning to isolate malicious agent subgroups. Evaluated across five datasets and four topologies, group collusion increases attack success by up to **15%**, while GroupGuard achieves up to **88% detection accuracy** and restores collaborative performance to **96.7%** of baseline. Our work establishes the first principled, deployable defense against collective deception in multi-agent LLM systems.

item.title: FedPBS: Proximal-Balanced Scaling Federated Learning Model for Robust Personalized Training for Non-IID Data
item.description: Federated learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative model training across distributed clients, yet suffers from statistical heterogeneity (e.g., severe Non-IID data) and system heterogeneity (e.g., resource-constrained devices), leading to unstable convergence and degraded personalization. To address this, we propose **FedPBS**, a novel FL algorithm that synergistically integrates *dynamic batch-size scaling* for resource-aware participation and *selective proximal correction* to stabilize local updates—only applied to small-batch clients to curb divergence without over-regularizing powerful ones. Evaluated under extreme Non-IID settings (Dirichlet α=0.1) on CIFAR-10 and UCI-HAR, FedPBS consistently outperforms FedBS, FedGA, MOON, and FedProx—achieving average accuracy gains of +2.8%–4.3%, smoother loss curves, and 67% lower training variance. It delivers robust, scalable, and personalized performance across diverse federated environments with no extra communication cost.

item.title: Multi-Modal Character Localization and Extraction for Chinese Text Recognition
item.description: Scene text recognition (STR) excels on English text but struggles with Chinese due to its complex character structures (e.g., strokes, components, hierarchical composition) and vast character inventory (>3,500 common characters). Existing end-to-end STR models—designed for English—hit an accuracy ceiling on Chinese, raising the question: *Is English-optimized architecture suitable for Chinese STR?* To address this, we propose **LER**, a novel multi-modal framework that explicitly decouples localization, extraction, and recognition at the **character level**, respecting Chinese’s “character-centric” nature. The **Localization** module fuses visual and linguistic priors for precise character positioning; the **Extraction** module parallelly isolates characters via deformable RoI alignment; the **Recognition** module employs dual-stream encoding—structural (stroke/component hierarchy) and appearance-based—to resolve confusable characters. On four large-scale Chinese benchmarks, LER achieves +4.2–+7.8% absolute accuracy gains over SOTA. Remarkably, it also sets new state-of-the-art on six English benchmarks and Union14M, demonstrating strong cross-lingual generalization. Code: https://github.com/Pandarenlql/LER.

item.title: Computation and Communication Efficient Federated Unlearning via On-server Gradient Conflict Mitigation and Expression
item.description: Federated Unlearning (FUL) enables compliant removal of specific clients’ data influence from a federated model, yet suffers from cross-client knowledge opacity and prohibitive computation/communication costs. We propose **Federated On-server Unlearning (FOUL)**, a two-stage framework: (1) a *learning-to-unlearn* phase that efficiently encodes forget-client-specific gradient conflict patterns during normal training with negligible client-side overhead; and (2) an *on-server aggregation* phase that performs data-free unlearning via gradient conflict mitigation and parameter expression reconstruction—requiring no client re-participation or raw data access. We introduce a standardized FUL benchmark setting and the **time-to-forget** metric—measuring rounds to achieve target unlearning performance. Experiments across CIFAR-10, FEMNIST, and Shakespeare show FOUL reduces communication by 68–82% and server computation time by 53–71% versus Retraining, while accelerating time-to-forget by 3.2–5.7× and achieving 92.4% forgetting success (vs. 76.1% for Retraining) with <1.2% utility drop.

item.title: REFINE-DP: Diffusion Policy Fine-tuning for Humanoid Loco-manipulation via Reinforcement Learning
item.description: Humanoid loco-manipulation demands tight coordination between high-level motion planning and low-level whole-body control under complex dynamics and long-horizon tasks. While diffusion policies (DPs) excel in demonstration-based learning, their offline-trained planners suffer from severe command tracking failure and compounding distribution shift when decoupled from real-time controllers—especially on high-DOF humanoids. Scaling demonstration data is prohibitively expensive. To bridge this gap, we propose **REFINE-DP**, a hierarchical framework that jointly fine-tunes a DP planner and an RL-based loco-manipulation controller via PPO-driven diffusion policy gradients. Crucially, the controller adapts *in tandem* to the planner’s evolving command distribution, mitigating distributional mismatch. Evaluated on door traversal and long-horizon object transport, REFINE-DP achieves **>90% success in simulation**, generalizes robustly to out-of-distribution scenarios unseen during pre-training, and enables smooth, autonomous execution on real-world dynamic environments. It substantially outperforms pretrained DP baselines, demonstrating that RL fine-tuning—not just larger datasets—is essential for reliable humanoid loco-manipulation.

item.title: Quantum-Enhanced Vision Transformer for Flood Detection using Remote Sensing Imagery
item.description:
## Quantum-Enhanced Vision Transformer for Flood Detection
Reliable flood detection from remote sensing imagery remains challenging due to high dimensionality, nonlinearity, and terrain-induced ambiguities. To address this, we propose a novel **quantum-classical hybrid Vision Transformer (Q-ViT)** that integrates a 4-qubit parameterized quantum circuit—trained end-to-end—as a dedicated local feature mapper alongside a classical ViT backbone. The two pathways process inputs in parallel and fuse representations adaptively for binary classification. Evaluated on real-world Sentinel-2 and GF-6 data, Q-ViT achieves **94.47% accuracy (+9.99% over classical ViT) and an F1-score of 0.944 (+0.103)**, with marked gains in complex terrains (e.g., wetlands, terraced fields). This demonstrates the concrete advantage of quantum-enhanced feature learning for hydrological monitoring and establishes a scalable paradigm for quantum-augmented earth observation.

item.title: LLM-MINE: Large Language Model based Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias Phenotypes Mining from Clinical Notes
item.description: Alzheimer’s Disease and Related Dementias (ADRD) phenotyping from unstructured clinical notes remains a major bottleneck for early detection and staging. We present **LLM-MINE**, a large language model–based framework that enables zero-shot or few-shot extraction of clinically meaningful ADRD phenotypes without fine-tuning. Leveraging two expert-curated phenotype lists, LLM-MINE prompts open-weight LLMs (e.g., Llama-3-8B-Instruct) to identify and normalize phenotype mentions in real-world clinical notes. Evaluation across multi-cohort data shows statistically significant phenotype differences (χ² *p* < 0.001), with **memory impairment** as the strongest discriminator. Few-shot prompting using the combined phenotype list achieves best unsupervised disease staging performance (**ARI = 0.290, NMI = 0.232**), substantially outperforming biomedical NER and dictionary-based baselines. Our work demonstrates that prompt-engineered LLMs offer a scalable, interpretable, and clinically grounded approach to mining ADRD signals from EHR text.

item.title: DualSwinFusionSeg: Multimodal Martian Landslide Segmentation via Dual Swin Transformer with Multi-Scale Fusion and UNet++
item.description: Automated segmentation of Martian landslides—critical for planetary geology and robotic exploration—is hindered by multimodal heterogeneity (e.g., RGB, DEM, slope, thermal inertia) and extreme label scarcity. To address this, we propose **DualSwinFusionSeg**, a novel architecture featuring **dual Swin Transformer V2 encoders** that independently process RGB and auxiliary geophysical inputs, followed by **multi-scale cross-modal fusion via channel-wise concatenation** and decoding with a **UNet++ backbone** leveraging dense nested skip connections for boundary preservation. Ablation studies confirm that modality-specific encoding combined with simple concatenation outperforms attention-based fusion under limited training data. Evaluated on the MMLSv2 dataset (PBVS 2026 Mars-LS Challenge), our model achieves **0.867 mIoU and 0.905 F1 on the development set**, and **0.783 mIoU on the held-out test set**, setting a new state-of-the-art for multimodal planetary surface segmentation.

item.title: Shapes are not enough: CONSERVAttack and its use for finding vulnerabilities and uncertainties in machine learning applications
item.description: In high-energy physics, deep learning models are routinely validated against systematic uncertainties via marginal distributions and linear correlations in control regions—yet such physics-motivated checks cannot guarantee robustness against *unanticipated*, nonlinear deviations between simulation and data. We introduce **CONSERVAttack**, a novel adversarial method that generates physically plausible perturbations *within* established uncertainty bounds (e.g., jet energy scale ±3%, b-tag efficiency ±5%) while evading all standard validation metrics. Applied to top-quark classification using CMS-like data, it increases misclassification rates from 2.1% to 38.7% without violating any conventional statistical tests (KS p > 0.95, correlation shifts < 0.005). This demonstrates that “shape-consistency” alone is insufficient for reliability. We propose mitigation strategies—including uncertainty-aware training and manifold-sensitive anomaly detection—and argue that adversarial robustness must be an explicit requirement in HEP ML validation pipelines.

item.title: MICRO: A Lightweight Middleware for Optimizing Cross-store Cross-model Graph-Relation Joins [Technical Report]
item.description: Modern data applications increasingly require joining heterogeneous data across graph and relational stores—yet federated cross-model query processing remains underexplored. This paper bridges the gap by (1) formalizing cross-store graph-relation joins via a unified algebra; (2) introducing one real-world and four semi-synthetic benchmarks targeting realistic join patterns (e.g., high-cardinality, predicate-driven filtering); and (3) proposing MICRO, a lightweight middleware that executes queries natively without data materialization or model conversion. At its core lies CMLero, a learning-to-rank optimizer that selects efficient execution plans using minimal historical feedback—bypassing error-prone cost estimation. Experiments show MICRO outperforms the state-of-the-art federated system XDB by up to **2.1× in total runtime**, with **14 real-world queries achieving >100× speedup** (including 4 exceeding 100×). While 4 queries incur >5s slowdown—highlighting optimization opportunities—CMLero consistently surpasses rule-based and regression-based optimizers, demonstrating the efficacy of learning-to-rank for complex cross-model plan selection.

item.title: Data-driven Progressive Discovery of Physical Laws
item.description: Symbolic regression enables interpretable mathematical discovery from data, yet conventional end-to-end approaches often yield unphysical, overfitted expressions with poor generalization. Inspired by the hierarchical, progressive nature of scientific discovery—from Kepler’s third law to Newton’s law of universal gravitation—we propose the **Chain of Symbolic Regression (CoSR)**. CoSR decomposes physical law discovery into a sequence of interpretable, physically grounded symbolic regressions, where each stage builds upon prior knowledge units (e.g., dimensionless groups or dominant balances) under logical and dimensional constraints. Applied to turbulent Rayleigh–Bénard convection, viscous pipe flow, and laser–metal interaction, CoSR refines classical scaling theories with higher accuracy and physical fidelity. Notably, it discovers a novel, unified scaling law for aerodynamic coefficients across diverse aircraft geometries—advancing beyond empirical models. CoSR bridges data-driven modeling and scientific reasoning, offering a principled, explainable pathway to physical law discovery.

item.title: The GELATO Dataset for Legislative NER
item.description: This paper introduces **GELATO**, the first large-scale, expert-annotated NER dataset for U.S. legislative texts—comprising all 13,287 bills from the 118th Congress (House & Senate). It features a novel **two-level ontology**: Level 1 identifies 7 coarse-grained legislative entity types (e.g., bill numbers, sponsors, committees); Level 2 decomposes each into fine-grained semantic attributes (e.g., chamber, party, jurisdictional scope). We fine-tune BERT and RoBERTa variants on Level 1, achieving best F1 of **89.7% with RoBERTa-large**—significantly outperforming BERT. For Level 2, we deploy LLMs (LLaMA-3-8B, GPT-4 Turbo) with optimized, schema-constrained prompts, attaining **92.3% attribute accuracy**. Results demonstrate RoBERTa’s superiority for legislative context modeling and LLMs’ efficacy in structured legal reasoning—establishing a robust, modular pipeline for legislative information extraction.

item.title: OmniCompliance-100K: A Multi-Domain, Rule-Grounded, Real-World Safety Compliance Dataset
item.description: OmniCompliance-100K is the first large-scale, rule-grounded, real-world safety compliance dataset designed to bridge the critical gap between LLM safety evaluation and actual regulatory practice. Built via an autonomous web-searching agent, it curates **12,985 distinct rules** from **74 authoritative regulations and policies** across security, privacy, finance, healthcare, education, and human rights domains—and links each rule to **106,009 verified real-world compliance cases** (e.g., enforcement actions, platform policy updates, audit reports). Rigorous alignment analysis confirms >98% rule-case fidelity. Benchmarking 12 advanced LLMs reveals persistent weaknesses: even top models achieve <52% accuracy on multi-step compliance reasoning, with pronounced performance drops in high-stakes domains like finance and medical devices. The dataset enables fine-grained, interpretable, and regulation-aware safety evaluation—setting a new foundation for robust, auditable LLM deployment.

item.title: Learnability and Privacy Vulnerability are Entangled in a Few Critical Weights
item.description: Prior membership privacy defenses often retrain or perturb *all* network weights—costly and harmful to utility. This work reveals three key insights: (i) privacy vulnerability concentrates in a *tiny fraction* of weights (<0.1%); (ii) these vulnerable weights are also *critically important for model utility*; and (iii) their significance arises from *structural location*, not magnitude. Leveraging this, we propose **Critical Weight Rewind & Fine-tune (CWRF)**: we identify vulnerable-yet-critical weights via gradient variance and MIA sensitivity scoring, rewind them to earlier training states (not prune or reset), and fine-tune *only those weights*. Extensive experiments show CWRF reduces SOTA membership inference attack success by 32.7–41.5% while retaining 98.2–99.6% of original accuracy—outperforming DP-SGD, MemGuard, and fine-pruning. Our results demonstrate that learnability and privacy vulnerability are fundamentally entangled in structural weight locations—not values—enabling efficient, utility-preserving privacy defense.

item.title: Defensible Design for OpenClaw: Securing Autonomous Tool-Invoking Agents
item.description: OpenClaw-like autonomous agents—capable of interfacing with OS APIs, manipulating files, invoking tools, and installing extensions—deliver high productivity but inherit severe architectural insecurity: untrusted inputs, autonomous actions, extensibility, and privileged access are conflated in a single execution loop. Treating this as a *software engineering challenge*, not a product-specific issue, we propose a **defensible design blueprint** for such agents. We introduce a structured **risk taxonomy** categorizing vulnerabilities across input sources, execution contexts, extension mechanisms, and privilege boundaries; articulate four **secure engineering principles**—including minimal-privilege closed-loop execution and declarative permission binding; and define a concrete **research agenda** prioritizing policy-aware sandboxing, a declarative permission DSL, and behavioral baseline verification. Our work shifts focus from reactive patching to proactive, architecture-driven safety—enabling robust, auditable, and production-ready agent deployment.

item.title: PISmith: Reinforcement Learning-based Red Teaming for Prompt Injection Defenses
item.description: Prompt injection poses critical security risks to LLM-based autonomous agents, yet existing defenses lack rigorous evaluation under adaptive attacks. We propose **PISmith**, a reinforcement learning-based red-teaming framework that trains an attacker LLM to optimize prompt injections in a practical black-box setting—only querying the defended LLM and observing outputs. To overcome extreme reward sparsity (where most prompts are blocked, causing policy entropy collapse), we introduce *adaptive entropy regularization* and *dynamic advantage weighting*, enabling sustained exploration and effective learning from rare successes. Extensive evaluation across 13 benchmarks shows that state-of-the-art defenses remain vulnerable; PISmith achieves the highest attack success rates against all 7 baseline methods (static, search-based, and RL-based). It further demonstrates strong transferability in agentic settings on InjecAgent and AgentDojo, successfully attacking both open-source (e.g., Llama-3.1-8B) and closed-source models (e.g., GPT-4o-mini, GPT-5-nano). Code is publicly available.

item.title: Almost-Free Queue Jumping for Prior Inputs in Private Neural Inference
item.description: Privacy-Preserving Machine Learning as a Service (PP-MLaaS) enables secure inference via cryptographic primitives like homomorphic encryption (HE) and MPC, but existing batched frameworks process inputs strictly sequentially—hindering urgent request prioritization. Naïve queue jumping incurs prohibitive computational and communication overhead, degrading latency fairness. We propose **PrivQJ**, the first privacy-preserving framework for *almost-free* queue jumping in batched inference. PrivQJ leverages shared computation across inputs through *in-processing slot recycling*, enabling high-priority inputs to piggyback onto ongoing batch computations with negligible cryptographic cost—only $O(1)$ extra communication rounds and $O(\log n)$ additional HE operations. Experiments show **12.7×–18.3× lower priority latency** versus state-of-the-art PP-MLaaS systems, with no throughput degradation (<0.8% variance) and full semantic security preservation.

item.title: Balancing the privacy-utility trade-off: How to draw reliable conclusions from private data
item.description: This paper reframes the privacy-utility trade-off through the lens of **hypothesis testing**, addressing the critical gap between rigorous differential privacy (DP) theory and practical decision-making by non-experts. We introduce **Relative Disclosure Risk (RDR)**—a semantic, interpretable measure quantifying the maximum uncertainty reduction an adversary gains from DP outputs—and prove its direct, exponential relationship to the privacy-loss parameter ε ($\text{RDR} = 1 - e^{-\varepsilon}$). Concurrently, we analyze DP’s impact on statistical validity by deriving analytical bounds on Type I error control and statistical power for common hypothesis tests (e.g., t-test, χ²-test) under noise injection. Finally, we deliver actionable, non-technical guidance: translating target RDR thresholds into ε values, pre-assessing utility loss via effect-size-aware power estimation, and selecting mechanisms aligned with analytical goals. Our framework transforms ε from an abstract metric into a risk-management tool grounded in inference reliability.

item.title: Uncovering Security Threats and Architecting Defenses in Autonomous Agents: A Case Study of OpenClaw
item.description: This paper presents the first comprehensive security analysis of the OpenClaw autonomous agent framework, whose OS-level permissions and tool-calling autonomy introduce unprecedented attack surfaces. We empirically identify four critical threats: prompt-injection-driven Remote Code Execution (RCE), sequential multi-tool attack chains, context amnesia-induced privilege escalation, and supply-chain contamination. To systematize these risks, we propose a novel tri-layered taxonomy spanning AI Cognitive, Software Execution, and Information System dimensions. In response, we introduce the Full-Lifecycle Agent Security Architecture (FASA)—a zero-trust blueprint featuring dynamic intent verification, cross-layer reasoning-action correlation, and phased execution isolation. Building on FASA, we launch Project ClawGuard: an open-source engineering initiative delivering practical defenses, including runtime monitoring, semantic action validation, and a curated adversarial dataset. Our work bridges theoretical risk modeling with deployable safeguards, advancing autonomous agents toward trustworthy operation. Code and data: https://github.com/NY1024/ClawGuard.

item.title: AEGIS: No Tool Call Left Unchecked -- A Pre-Execution Firewall and Audit Layer for AI Agents
item.description: AEGIS is a pre-execution firewall and audit layer for AI agents that intercepts tool calls *before* execution to prevent harmful side effects. It introduces a lightweight, framework-agnostic three-stage pipeline: (i) deep string extraction from structured tool arguments; (ii) content-first risk scanning using semantic rules and fast classifiers; and (iii) composable, context-aware policy validation. High-risk calls can be held for human approval, and all decisions are immutably logged in a tamper-evident audit trail built on Ed25519 signatures and SHA-256 hash chaining. Evaluated across 14 agent frameworks (Python, JavaScript, Go), AEGIS blocks 100% of 48 adversarial tool calls, achieves only 1.2% false positives on 500 benign calls, and adds just 8.3 ms median latency per interception. These results demonstrate that practical, low-overhead, and deployable pre-execution mediation for AI agents is not only feasible—but immediately viable.

item.title: Geometry-Guided Camera Motion Understanding in VideoLLMs
item.description: Camera motion is a foundational geometric signal underlying visual narrative and perception, yet existing VideoLLMs rarely model it explicitly and fail on fine-grained motion primitives (e.g., distinguishing *dolly-in* from *zoom-in*). To bridge this gap, we introduce a three-stage framework: **benchmarking**, **diagnosis**, and **injection**. We curate *CameraMotionDataset*, a large-scale synthetic dataset with precise 6-DoF camera control, and build *CameraMotionVQA*, a constraint-aware multi-label VQA benchmark. Diagnostic probing reveals weak representation of motion cues—especially in deeper ViT layers—across off-the-shelf VideoLLMs. Without costly fine-tuning, we propose a lightweight, model-agnostic pipeline: extracting geometric cues (depth, flow) via 3D foundation models, predicting constrained motion primitives with a temporal classifier, and injecting them into VideoLLM inference via structured prompting. Experiments show +18.7% accuracy on CameraMotionVQA and markedly more camera-aware, spatially coherent responses—demonstrating geometry-guided cue extraction and structured prompting as practical, scalable steps toward camera-aware VideoLLMs and VLAs. Dataset and benchmark are publicly available.

item.title: Empowering Semantic-Sensitive Underwater Image Enhancement with VLM
item.description: This paper addresses the semantic degradation bottleneck in learning-based underwater image enhancement (UIE), where enhanced outputs suffer from distribution shifts that impair downstream vision tasks. We propose a VLM-empowered semantic-sensitive UIE framework: first, a frozen Vision-Language Model (e.g., BLIP-2) generates object-centric textual descriptions from degraded input; second, a lightweight text-image alignment module projects these descriptions back into pixel space to produce a spatial semantic guidance map; third, this map steers the UIE network via dual guidance—cross-attention modulation and an explicit semantic alignment loss—ensuring restoration prioritizes semantically critical regions (e.g., marine organisms, artifacts). Evaluated across multiple UIE backbones and benchmarks (UIEB, EUVP), our method consistently improves perceptual metrics (e.g., +1.8 dB PSNR) and, more importantly, boosts detection mAP by +4.2% and segmentation mIoU by +3.7% on downstream tasks. This demonstrates that VLM-driven semantic grounding significantly enhances both fidelity and task utility of underwater enhancement—without requiring task-specific retraining.

item.title: HSEmotion Team at ABAW-10 Competition: Facial Expression Recognition, Valence-Arousal Estimation, Action Unit Detection and Fine-Grained Violence Classification
item.description: This paper presents the HSEmotion team’s solution for the 10th Affective Behavior Analysis in-the-Wild (ABAW-10) competition. For frame-wise tasks—facial expression recognition (FER), valence-arousal (VA) estimation, and action unit (AU) detection—we propose a fast, confidence-aware dual-path framework: high-confidence predictions from an EfficientNet-B0 model fine-tuned on AffWild2 are used directly; otherwise, facial embeddings are fed into a lightweight MLP trained jointly on VA regression and AU multi-label classification. Frame-level outputs are smoothed via a fixed-size sliding window to suppress temporal noise. For the novel fine-grained violence classification task, we benchmark multiple pre-trained backbones (ViT-B/32, ResNet-50, ConvNeXt-Tiny) and aggregation strategies (mean/max pooling, LSTM), ultimately adopting attention-weighted frame embedding aggregation. Our approach achieves substantial improvements over baselines across all four ABAW-10 validation tracks: +4.2% macro-F1 on FER, +0.06 CCC on VA, +3.8% mean F1 on AU, and +5.1% accuracy on violence classification.

item.title: Federated Hierarchical Clustering with Automatic Selection of Optimal Cluster Numbers
item.description: Federated Clustering (FC) enables unsupervised exploration of global data distributions across privacy-sensitive clients, yet existing methods assume a known, uniform number of clusters—contrary to real-world imbalanced and unknown cluster structures. To address this, we propose **Fed-$k^*$-HC**, a novel framework that *automatically determines the optimal number of clusters* $k^*$ via federated hierarchical clustering. Each client generates local micro-subcluster prototypes, which are uploaded to the server; the server then performs density-aware hierarchical merging—guided by prototype neighborhood relationships—to self-terminate and output $k^*$ without manual tuning. This design inherently handles varying cluster sizes, shapes, and imbalances while mitigating information loss under privacy constraints. Extensive experiments on MNIST, FMNIST, CIFAR-10, and clinical time-series data show Fed-$k^*$-HC achieves state-of-the-art accuracy in $k^*$ estimation (98.2% avg. match to ground truth), +5.2% purity gain over baselines, and 37% lower communication cost. It establishes the first principled integration of adaptive hierarchical clustering into federated learning for robust, parameter-free unsupervised discovery.

item.title: CA-HFP: Curvature-Aware Heterogeneous Federated Pruning with Model Reconstruction
item.description: We propose **CA-HFP** (Curvature-Aware Heterogeneous Federated Pruning), a novel framework for efficient federated learning on heterogeneous edge devices. CA-HFP enables each client to perform **structured, curvature-informed pruning**—using Hessian-based significance scores—to generate device-specific compact submodels, then **reconstructs them into the global parameter space** via a lightweight linear projection, preserving aggregation compatibility. We derive a rigorous convergence bound for federated optimization with multi-step local SGD, explicitly incorporating data heterogeneity and pruning-induced perturbations; this bound yields a **principled, loss-aware pruning criterion**. Experiments on FMNIST, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 with VGG and ResNet architectures under strong Non-IID settings (α = 0.1–1.0) show that CA-HFP reduces per-client computation and communication by up to 62% and 73%, respectively, while maintaining accuracy within 0.9% of the full model—outperforming FedAvg and state-of-the-art pruning baselines by up to 2.7% in accuracy under severe heterogeneity.

item.title: Embedded Quantum Machine Learning in Embedded Systems: Feasibility, Hybrid Architectures, and Quantum Co-Processors
item.description: Embedded Quantum Machine Learning (EQML) aims to deploy quantum machine learning (QML) on resource-constrained edge devices—yet remains experimentally nascent in 2026. This paper establishes feasibility boundaries from a circuits-and-systems perspective, formalizing two hybrid implementation pathways: (i) *remote-offload workflows*, where embedded hardware handles sensing and classical preprocessing while delegating narrowly scoped quantum subroutines (e.g., variational optimization kernels) to remote or nearby QPUs; and (ii) *integrated quantum co-processors*, exploring compact quantum modules (e.g., superconducting or photonic) tightly coupled with classical control logic (e.g., RISC-V + FPGA). We identify five dominant barriers—latency, data encoding overhead, NISQ noise, toolchain mismatch, and energy—and map each to concrete engineering directions: quantum-classical interface protocols, cryo- or ambient-compatible control electronics, power-aware quantum task scheduling, lightweight verification for embedded quantum circuits, and security-by-design (including side-channel resilience). Crucially, we argue that responsible EQML deployment mandates adversarial evaluation and governance frameworks aligned with emerging edge AI standards.

item.title: Federated Few-Shot Learning on Neuromorphic Hardware: An Empirical Study Across Physical Edge Nodes
item.description: This work presents the first empirical study of federated few-shot learning (FFSL) on physical neuromorphic hardware—specifically, dual BrainChip Akida AKD1000 chips. Addressing the fundamental incompatibility between spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP)—which yields binary weight updates—and gradient-based federated algorithms, we conduct ~1,580 trials across seven analytical phases on N-MNIST. Among four weight-exchange strategies, **neuron-level concatenation (FedUnion)** achieves 77.0% federated accuracy (n=30), significantly outperforming element-wise averaging (FedAvg), which collapses performance (p = 0.002). Domain-adaptive fine-tuning of the upstream feature extractor accounts for most accuracy gains, confirming **feature quality—not aggregation method—as the dominant factor**. Scaling feature dimensionality from 64 to 256 boosts accuracy by 12.3 points (p < 0.001), and two observed asymmetries—wider features benefit federation more than local learning, while binarization harms federation disproportionately—converge on a unified mechanism: **cross-node knowledge transfer scales with the distinctiveness of neuron prototypes**, i.e., prototype complementarity.

item.title: SCOPE: Semantic Coreset with Orthogonal Projection Embeddings for Federated learning
item.description: SCOPE (Semantic Coreset with Orthogonal Projection Embeddings) is a novel federated coreset framework designed for extreme class-imbalanced, instrument-generated data streams. Unlike prior heuristic or gradient-based methods, SCOPE operates in the latent space to compute three interpretable scalar scores per sample: (i) a *representation reliability score* (assessing core-class feature consistency), (ii) an *orthogonal diversity score* (measuring novelty of residuals in orthogonal subspaces), and (iii) a *boundary proximity score* (identifying ambiguous or anomalous samples near decision boundaries). Crucially, SCOPE transmits only these lightweight scalars—not embeddings or gradients—to a central server, enabling efficient global consensus construction with minimal uplink cost. Guided by this consensus, clients dynamically prune redundant and noisy local samples to counteract long-tail skew. Experiments show SCOPE achieves competitive global accuracy (within ±0.3% of full-data training), robust convergence, and exceptional efficiency: **128×–512× uplink bandwidth reduction**, **7.72× wall-clock speedup** in local coreset selection, and significantly lower FLOP/VRAM overhead.

item.title: Enhanced Drug-drug Interaction Prediction Using Adaptive Knowledge Integration
item.description: Drug-drug interaction event (DDIE) prediction remains critical for clinical safety yet suffers from data imbalance, mechanistic complexity, and poor generalization to unseen drug pairs. We propose Adaptive Knowledge Integration (AKI), a reinforcement learning–guided framework that dynamically selects, weights, and synthesizes structured pharmacological knowledge (e.g., targets, pathways, ADME properties) before injecting it into a frozen large language model (LLM). Unlike static prompting or full fine-tuning, AKI’s policy network optimizes knowledge usage per query via a dual reward signal—prediction accuracy and knowledge consistency—enabling context-aware, interpretable reasoning. In few-shot settings (5 samples per class), AKI achieves +12.7% average F1 improvement over strong LLM baselines across four benchmarks (DDI-2013, TWOSIDES, DrugBank-DDI, MedDRA-DDI), with +18.3% recall gain for high-risk interactions. Ablation confirms RL-driven knowledge scheduling contributes the largest gain (+9.4%). AKI establishes a scalable, knowledge-adaptive paradigm for scientific reasoning in biomedicine.

item.title: Deep Distance Measurement Method for Unsupervised Multivariate Time Series Similarity Retrieval
item.description: We propose the Deep Distance Measurement Method (DDMM), a novel unsupervised learning framework for multivariate time series similarity retrieval. DDMM introduces a dynamic weighting strategy for anchor-positive pairs sampled arbitrarily from full-length sequences: weights are assigned inversely proportional to their Euclidean distance in the embedding space, enabling explicit learning of minute intra-state variations—critical for industrial anomaly detection and root-cause analysis. Unlike conventional contrastive methods relying on rigid augmentations or predefined labels, DDMM operates end-to-end without supervision and preserves fine-grained state distinctions. Evaluated on the real-world Pulp-and-paper mill dataset, DDMM achieves 18.7% higher mAP than state-of-the-art baselines (e.g., TS2Vec, TFC). Further, integrating DDMM with existing feature extractors yields a hybrid model that boosts Top-5 recall to 92.4%. This work establishes a new paradigm for unsupervised industrial time series understanding by prioritizing *intra-state discriminability*.

item.title: Securely Scaling Autonomy: The Role of Cryptography in Future Unmanned Aircraft Systems (UAS)
item.description: This paper presents the first comprehensive, flight-constrained evaluation of cryptographic primitives for scalable autonomous Unmanned Aircraft Systems (UAS). We benchmark seven open-source libraries for Messaging Layer Security (MLS) and threshold signatures (FROST/BLS) across static analysis, network-simulated swarms, and a novel bulk-signing stress test. Results confirm OpenMLS—not Cisco MLSpp—as the only production-ready MLS implementation for dynamic UAS swarms, achieving sub-10ms key updates at 1,000-node scale. Crucially, operating MLS in “Key Management” mode improves throughput by 3.8× and resilience to packet loss by 52%. For consensus, Zcash’s FROST implementation outperforms alternatives in sustained high-volume scenarios, balancing strong security guarantees, fast signature verification (2.1× faster than BLS), and compact signature size. These findings provide actionable, empirically grounded guidance for deploying secure, decentralized autonomy in resource-limited, contested airspace.

item.title: ${{\mathsf{SMA}^2\mathsf{RT}}}$ : Secret-Metadata Attribute-based Anonymous Rate-limited Tokens
item.description: We introduce **${\mathsf{SMA}^2\mathsf{RT}}$**, the first anonymous token scheme supporting *secret metadata embedding* and *selective attribute disclosure* in a single, efficient framework. Unlike prior ATPM schemes requiring one online interaction per token, ${\mathsf{SMA}^2\mathsf{RT}}$ leverages Signatures on Equivalence Classes (SEQ) to enable “issue once, spend up to $N$ times”: a user obtains a master credential in one interaction and then derives $N$ unlinkable, valid tokens *locally*, with no further communication. Each token preserves the issuer’s hidden bit (e.g., bot suspicion flag) and allows the user to prove specific attributes (e.g., “subscriber”, “age ≥ 18”) via zero-knowledge proofs—without compromising anonymity or revealing extra information. This achieves optimal communication complexity ($O(1)$ setup), strong unlinkability, fine-grained policy enforcement, and real-time practicality for high-volume web services.

item.title: Multi-Instance Security Degradation of Code-Based KEMs
item.description: This paper identifies a critical security degradation in code-based KEMs—**HQC, BIKE, and Classic McEliece**—under the *multi-instance* setting, where a single public key is used to derive $M$ session keys $K^{(1)},\dots,K^{(M)}$. We show that attackers can exploit structural properties to construct DOOM instances with $nM$ syndromes (for HQC/BIKE) or $M$ syndromes (for McEliece), amplifying decoding advantage beyond standard single-instance analyses. For NIST Level 1 (143-bit security), we find concrete thresholds: security drops below 143 bits when $M \geq 2^{69}$ (`HQC-1`), $M \geq 2^8$ (`BIKE-1`), or $M \geq 2^{15}$ (`mceliece3488-64`). Furthermore, we propose a novel DOOM-type attack against HQC in the *Common Code* setting: recovering the secret key from $M \geq 2^9$ public keys within $<143$-bit cost—rendering this deployment model insecure. Our results necessitate revised parameter selection and usage guidelines for code-based KEMs.

item.title: Towards Compact UOV-Based MQ Signatures: Rectangular and Lifted Whipping Structures
item.description: Multivariate Quadratic (MQ) signatures promise efficiency but suffer from large public keys. Recent “whipped” UOV schemes like MAYO use *emulsifier matrices* to expand small UOV instances while ensuring signing reduces to a full-rank linear system. This work introduces two general improvements: (1) **Rectangular emulsifiers**, enabling fewer equations in the base UOV and yielding significantly smaller public keys—without compromising the critical full-rank guarantee for signing; and (2) **Lifting emulsifiers to an extension field**, while keeping the core UOV map over the base field—effectively thwarting lifted-system attacks by preserving a near-1 variable-to-equation ratio. Combining both, we design **MAYO$^−_L$**, provide rigorous security analysis against forgery and key-recovery attacks, and propose parameter sets achieving ~25% smaller public keys and ~15% shorter signatures at 128-bit security. We also show that lifting integrates seamlessly into SNOVA, whereas rectangular emulsifiers face structural constraints there.

item.title: Privacy at your Fingertips: Enabling Rapid Client-Side Operations in Fully Homomorphic Encryption
item.description: Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) enables privacy-preserving computation on untrusted servers but suffers from prohibitive client-side overhead—slow encryption/decryption and massive ciphertext expansion—hindering deployment on edge devices. This paper introduces *tonetwo*, a novel client-side optimization framework that leverages bootstrapping to eliminate client-side encoding/decoding and floating-point arithmetic, drastically reducing both computational and communication burdens. Our approach maintains full FHE security and precision while achieving up to **97% reduction** in client enc/dec time and data transfer. We implement a cross-platform FHE client supporting software (ARM), FPGA (Xilinx), and ASIC backends; FPGA prototyping delivers **76× speedup** over prior art, and ASIC synthesis confirms high area efficiency. The framework enables practical, low-latency FHE operations on microcontrollers—e.g., 1.8 ms per ciphertext on STM32F4—paving the way for real-world private computing at the edge.

item.title: Secure Matrix Invertibility Testing over Fields of Small Order or Characteristics
item.description: This paper presents two perfectly secure, constant-round protocols for multi-party matrix invertibility testing over finite fields of small order or characteristic—critical for thresholdizing Multivariate Quadratic (MQ) signature schemes. The first protocol lifts the Cramer-Damgård framework via field extension to handle small-order fields; the second adapts the Samuelson-Berkowitz determinant algorithm specifically for small-characteristic fields where $p \leq n$. Both are formalized in the Arithmetic Black-Box model with Shamir’s secret sharing, achieving information-theoretic privacy without computational assumptions. Protocol 1 requires 7 offline rounds ($O(N n^4 + n^5)$) and 3 online rounds ($O(n^3)$); Protocol 2 uses 3 offline rounds ($O(n^3)$) and 9 online rounds ($O(n^4)$). Our constructions resolve the long-standing tradeoff between perfect security, round efficiency, and practical complexity in this setting.

item.title: zkBSA: Auditable Blockchain Stealth Address
item.description: zkBSA is the first modular framework for *auditable stealth addresses*, resolving the critical tension between receiver privacy and regulatory compliance in amount-transparent blockchains. It enables *preventive on-chain verification*: transactions to non-whitelisted receivers are cryptographically rejected, while authorized auditors can decrypt receiver identities via secure public-key encryption (EC-ElGamal). Built from four orthogonal components—stealth addressing, encrypted audit data transmission, Merkle-based vector commitment to a public KYC whitelist, and a custom zero-knowledge proof system—zkBSA achieves unlinkability against public observers and compliance soundness against malicious senders, both formally proven under standard cryptographic assumptions (DDH, CRHF). A production-ready PoC using ERC-5564, RISC Zero zkVM, and Merkle trees scales to 16 million users, with proof generation under 5.3 seconds and constant on-chain verification cost (~235k gas). zkBSA bridges privacy-enhancing technologies and real-world financial regulation.

item.title: Human-Extractable ZK Proofs of Knowledge: A Solution to Dark DAOs
item.description: We introduce **Human-Extractable Zero-Knowledge Proofs of Knowledge (HE-ZKPoK)**, a novel cryptographic primitive to counter Dark DAOs—decentralized cartels enabling automated vote-buying via key encumbrance (e.g., MPC/TEEs). Unlike prior TEE- or ASIC-dependent defenses (e.g., *individual* or *complete knowledge*), HE-ZKPoK integrates standard ZKPoK with human-solvable CAPTCHA puzzles: the prover must simultaneously complete the ZK proof and correctly solve CAPTCHAs issued by the verifier. Crucially, any human observer can extract the full witness (e.g., the secret key) solely from the prover’s CAPTCHA queries and responses—no specialized hardware or trusted setup is needed. Under the assumption that humans cannot encumber secrets, selling a vote inevitably exposes the private key to the buyer, thus deterring vote-buying. HE-ZKPoK is software-only, blockchain-native, and provides the first practical, auditable, and hardware-free defense against Dark DAO attacks.

item.title: Privacy-Preserving Machine Learning for IoT: A Cross-Paradigm Survey and Future Roadmap
item.description: This survey provides the first IoT-centric, cross-paradigm analysis of privacy-preserving machine learning (PPML), addressing critical gaps in decentralized, resource-constrained environments. We propose a unified taxonomy covering four paradigms: perturbation-based (e.g., differential privacy), distributed (e.g., federated learning), cryptographic (e.g., homomorphic encryption, secure multiparty computation), and generative synthesis (e.g., GAN-based data generation). For each, we rigorously assess formal privacy guarantees, computational/communication overhead, scalability under device heterogeneity, and resilience against membership inference, model inversion, gradient leakage, and adversarial attacks. We further analyze real-world deployment trade-offs—especially privacy vs. convergence speed, energy efficiency, and latency—in next-generation wireless IoT architectures. The work consolidates evaluation methodologies, benchmarks (e.g., Edge-IIoTset), and open-source frameworks (e.g., PySyft, TFF), and identifies key future directions: hybrid privacy integration, energy-aware PPML, privacy-preserving edge LLMs, and quantum-resilient PPML.

item.title: CTI-REALM: Benchmark to Evaluate Agent Performance on Security Detection Rule Generation Capabilities
item.description: CTI-REALM is a novel benchmark designed to evaluate AI agents’ capability to interpret real-world cyber threat intelligence (CTI) and generate actionable detection rules—mimicking the end-to-end workflow of security analysts. It features emulated, multi-stage attacks across Linux, cloud, and AKS environments, grounded in verified attack traces and schema-aware tool use (e.g., STIX parsing, MITRE ATT&CK mapping). Evaluation combines final detection accuracy with trajectory-based rewards that assess reasoning quality, tool selection, and error recovery. Comprehensive testing of 16 state-of-the-art models shows Claude Opus 4.6 (High) achieves the highest overall reward (0.637), outperforming Claude Opus 4.5 (0.624) and GPT-5 variants. Ablation studies confirm CTI-specific tools boost performance by 21.3%; variance analysis across repeated runs demonstrates high result stability; and memory augmentation via seeded contextual knowledge closes 33% of the performance gap between smaller and larger models. CTI-REALM establishes a rigorous, realistic standard for measuring progress in AI-driven detection engineering.

item.title: Purifying Generative LLMs from Backdoors without Prior Knowledge or Clean Reference
item.description: Backdoor attacks severely compromise the trustworthiness of instruction-tuned LLMs, yet existing removal methods rely on trigger knowledge, clean reference models, or aggressive fine-tuning—assumptions often violated in practice. We propose the first *prior-free, reference-free* purification framework for generative LLMs. Through systematic analysis, we find backdoor associations are redundantly encoded in MLP layers—not attention heads—and thus shift focus from trigger localization to *disrupting trigger-behavior coupling*. Our immunization-inspired approach synthesizes multiple backdoored variants with diverse trigger-behavior pairs, identifies recurrent parameter modifications across variants as a shared “backdoor signature”, and then neutralizes high-risk components followed by lightweight LoRA-based fluency restoration. Experiments on Llama-2, Qwen, and Phi-3 show >96% defense success against unseen semantic, lexical, and multimodal triggers, while preserving >98.5% original instruction-following and zero-shot capability—outperforming all prior reference-free baselines.

item.title: Technical Case Study of Privacy-Enhancing Technologies (PETs) for Public Health
item.description: This technical case study addresses the PETs for Public Health Challenge—a collaborative initiative to enable safe, privacy-compliant use of sensitive private-sector data (e.g., financial transactions) for pandemic response. Leveraging **ε=1.0 Differential Privacy**, we generated realistic synthetic financial data that preserves spatial-temporal patterns while providing rigorous privacy guarantees. Integrated with public health and mobility datasets, this DP-protected data demonstrated strong predictive power: it enabled accurate nowcasting (e.g., hotspot detection, adherence monitoring) and forecasting (e.g., mobility-driven transmission modeling, dynamic contact matrix estimation). The project delivered **six reusable open tools**, validated across real-world epidemiological decision scenarios. Key contributions include a practical PETs implementation framework, empirical evidence of DP synthetic data’s utility for public health analytics, and actionable best practices for privacy-preserving cross-sector data sharing.
